skill.name: organizing-with-labels
skill.description: Design and manage GitHub label taxonomies, bulk label operations, milestones, issue grouping, and progress tracking with GitHub CLI helper scripts.
skill.summary: Organize GitHub issues with consistent labels, milestones, and sprint tracking.
skill.author: C0ntr0lledCha0s

security_audit.summary: Static C2 and weak cryptography findings are false positives from GitHub label names and hex color values. The skill intentionally runs GitHub CLI commands that can create, edit, delete, and bulk assign repository labels or milestones. No evidence found of credential theft, hidden network exfiltration, prompt injection, or malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Repository-Changing GitHub CLI Automation
medium_findings.description: The helper scripts call gh commands that create, edit, delete, and bulk assign labels and milestones. This is intended behavior, but it can change repository state at scale.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess calls are direct evidence of mutating gh operations. They use argument lists rather than shell execution, so command injection evidence is not present.

low_findings.title: Bulk Cleanup Can Delete Labels After Confirmation
low_findings.description: The label cleanup flow can remove labels that are not in a preset after user confirmation. This is an operational safety concern, not malicious behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code clearly prompts before cleanup and deletion. Risk remains because confirmed actions can remove repository labels in bulk.

low_findings.title: Static Cryptography and C2 Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The cited asset entries are GitHub labels and hex color codes. No evidence found of cryptographic routines, command and control logic, or encoded payloads.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content consists of label taxonomy examples and color values. The semantic context does not support malware, C2, or weak cryptography findings.

low_findings.title: Documentation Includes Shell Command Examples
low_findings.description: The markdown files include gh, jq, xargs, and helper-script examples for users to run. They are transparent operational examples, not hidden execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are presented in documentation and match the skill purpose. They still warrant review because users may copy commands that mutate repository metadata.

dangerous_patterns.title: Python subprocess.run Invokes gh CLI
dangerous_patterns.description: The scripts use subprocess.run to call gh for repository metadata operations. Arguments are passed as lists, which reduces shell injection risk.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is present and security relevant. No shell=True or string-shell construction was found in the reviewed subprocess calls.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bulk Repository Metadata Mutation
dangerous_patterns.description: Bulk label and milestone commands can edit many issues or labels. Users should run dry runs and verify filters before applying changes.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code provides bulk operations and some dry-run or confirmation paths. The risk is operational impact from legitimate repository changes.

content.value_statement: Unstructured issues make triage, planning, and release tracking slow. This skill creates consistent labels, milestones, bulk updates, and progress reports for GitHub repositories.
actual_capabilities: Designs label taxonomies for type, priority, scope, status, and size categories.
Applies built-in standard, comprehensive, or minimal label presets.
Runs helper workflows for label creation, cleanup, bulk assignment, and consistency fixes.
Creates, updates, closes, lists, and tracks GitHub milestones.
Suggests labels from issue text using keyword-based inference.
Generates label usage and milestone progress summaries.
limitations: Requires GitHub CLI access and repository permissions for live changes.
Bulk operations can change many issues, labels, or milestones at once.
Label inference is keyword-based and may need human review.
Preset scopes are examples and may need customization for each project.

use_cases.title: Set Up a New Repository Taxonomy
use_cases.description: Create a clear label system before issues accumulate and triage becomes inconsistent.

use_cases.title: Clean Up Existing Labels
use_cases.description: Find duplicate, unused, or inconsistent labels and migrate issues to a simpler taxonomy.

use_cases.title: Track Sprint or Release Progress
use_cases.description: Create milestones, assign matching issues, and report completion by issue count and label.

prompt_templates.title: Create Basic Labels
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a small project.
prompt_templates.prompt: Set up a minimal GitHub label taxonomy for this repository. Explain each label before making changes.

prompt_templates.title: Apply a Standard Preset
prompt_templates.scenario: Use the built-in preset with review first.
prompt_templates.prompt: Preview the standard label preset for my repository. Show what will be created or changed before applying it.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Label Quality
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze my current GitHub labels for duplicates, unused labels, missing categories, and inconsistent naming.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Release Milestone
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a release milestone, find matching issues by label and search filter, and prepare a dry-run assignment plan.

output_examples.input: Set up labels for a new web app repository.
output: Recommended taxonomy: type labels, priority labels, and frontend, backend, API, and documentation scopes.
Suggested first action: preview the standard preset, then add project-specific scope labels.
Milestone summary with open and closed issue counts, due date status, and completion percentage.
Progress grouped by label so the team can see which areas are behind.

output_examples.input: Clean up labels that are not used.
output: A review list of unused labels and inconsistent names appears first.
Deletion or migration steps require confirmation before repository changes are made.

best_practices: Run dry-run previews before bulk label or milestone changes.
Use consistent prefixes such as priority, scope, status, and size.
Export current labels before cleanup or migration work.
anti_patterns: Do not create many overlapping labels for the same meaning.
Do not use labels for assignees, dates, or sprint numbers when GitHub has better fields.
Do not run cleanup commands without reviewing the affected labels and issues.

faq.question: Does this skill modify my repository?
faq.answer: Yes, it can modify labels, issues, and milestones when you run the helper commands with GitHub permissions.

faq.question: Can I preview changes first?
faq.answer: Yes. Several workflows support dry runs or review steps before applying bulk changes.

faq.question: What tools does it need?
faq.answer: It expects GitHub CLI access for live repository operations and normal file access for presets and reports.

faq.question: Can it design custom labels?
faq.answer: Yes. It can adapt type, priority, scope, status, and size labels to your project structure.

faq.question: Is label inference automatic?
faq.answer: It uses keyword matching to suggest labels. A person should review suggestions before applying them.

faq.question: Is this safe for large repositories?
faq.answer: Use dry runs, backups, and narrow filters. Bulk operations can affect many issues or labels quickly.
